channel.title: Hapi — Happy News by Region
channel.description: Uplifting stories from every corner of the world, organised by region.

item.title: Nigerian Scientist Rediscovers Rare Bat, Wins Global 'Green Nobel'
item.description: Conservation ecologist Iroro Tanshi rediscovered the short-tailed roundleaf bat in Nigeria's Afi Mountain Wildlife Sanctuary — the first sighting in the country in 45 years. Her community-led wildfire program now safeguards the habitat and protects 27,000 people's farms, earning her the 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize.

item.title: Madagascar Reforestation Corridor Shelters 12 Lemur Species
item.description: A new reforestation corridor in Madagascar is connecting fragmented forest patches and providing sanctuary for twelve or more lemur species found nowhere else on Earth. By linking habitats, conservationists are giving these endangered primates the space and genetic diversity they need to rebuild wild populations.

item.title: Sudan Orphanage Turns 180 War Orphans Into Future Peacemakers
item.description: A Sudanese orphanage is educating more than 180 children who lost parents to conflict, giving them shelter, schooling, and a sense of purpose: to become peacemakers. Staff report that graduates are already returning to their communities to mediate disputes and model reconciliation for younger children.

item.title: South Korean Student Wins Asia's First Youth-Led Climate Case
item.description: Borim Kim became the first person to win a youth-led climate litigation case through Asian courts, holding the government accountable for inadequate emissions targets. Now recognised with the Goldman Environmental Prize, Kim's landmark victory is inspiring a wave of young climate advocates across the continent.

item.title: Sri Lanka and Norway Team Up on Smarter, Cleaner Fishing
item.description: Sri Lanka's Fisheries Minister secured a landmark deal with Norway to integrate satellite-based vessel tracking, strengthening efforts to combat illegal fishing in the Indian Ocean. The partnership also invites Sri Lanka into Norway's Global Action Network on Sustainable Food, boosting research to improve the nutritional quality of fish for island families.

item.title: KL Rahul's Unbeaten 152 Shatters All-Time IPL Batting Record
item.description: KL Rahul hit an extraordinary unbeaten 152 — the highest individual score by an Indian in IPL history — sending 28,000 fans into raptures at Arun Jaitley Stadium. The innings headlined a week of record-breaking batting in IPL 2026 that has captivated cricket fans across Asia.

item.title: Germany's Coal Scars Become Europe's Largest Lake Landscape
item.description: Germany has completed its transformation of the Lusatian coal-mining region into 23 human-made lakes covering 14,000 hectares — Europe's largest artificial lake landscape. The final lake opened for swimming and boating this month, turning a post-industrial wound in the earth into a thriving recreational paradise.

item.title: Gene Therapy Gives UK 6-Year-Old the Gift of Sight
item.description: Six-year-old Saffie Sandford received pioneering Luxturna gene therapy at London's Great Ormond Street Hospital for a rare inherited form of blindness. Her family described the moment she ran outside and saw sunlight clearly for the first time as utterly life-changing — a triumph for both science and the NHS.

item.title: EU Offers 40,000 Free Rail Passes to Young Europeans
item.description: The European Union's Discover EU scheme is providing 40,000 free rail travel passes to 18- and 19-year-olds, inviting a new generation to explore the continent by train. The initiative is designed to build cross-cultural friendships and give young people a lived sense of their shared European identity.

item.title: Colorado Opens $43 Million Nature Library With Free Outdoor Gear
item.description: A spectacular new 33,000-square-foot library in Adams County, Colorado houses 35,000 books on ecology and the outdoors, plus free TryIt Kits stuffed with fishing poles, tents, and backpacks. The Anythink Libraries branch is designed to ensure that access to nature is never a matter of wealth.

item.title: US Road Deaths Hit Lowest Level Since 2019
item.description: Traffic fatalities in the United States fell to 36,640 in 2025 — a 6.7% decline and the fourth consecutive annual drop. The fatality rate per mile driven reached its second-lowest level in recorded history, reflecting gains in vehicle safety technology, road design improvements, and better enforcement.

item.title: US Renewables Beat Natural Gas for an Entire Month — a First
item.description: In March 2026, wind, solar, hydro, and biomass generated more electricity across the United States than natural gas for a full calendar month for the very first time. Emissions-free sources provided over half of the nation's electricity, reaching that milestone for only the third time in history.

item.title: Medellín Neighbourhood's Flood Fixes Adopted for Entire City
item.description: The Golondrinas neighbourhood in Medellín designed eight low-cost climate adaptations — from rainwater management to eco-gardens — to protect its hillside streets from flooding and landslides. City officials were so impressed they are scaling the solutions to serve all four million of Medellín's residents.

item.title: Ecuador Study: Rainforest Biodiversity Bounces Back 90% in 30 Years
item.description: Scientists analysing over 8,500 species in Ecuadorian secondary forests found that tropical biodiversity rebounds to more than 90% of its original richness within just 30 years once land is left to recover. Birds and mammals, by dispersing seeds and pollinating plants, are the powerful engines driving this remarkable regeneration.

item.title: Colombian Activist Halts Commercial Fracking, Wins Green Nobel
item.description: Yuvelis Morales Blanco spent years mobilising her community in Colombia against commercial fracking and won, securing protections for local water supplies, farmland, and Indigenous territories. Awarded the 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize, her grassroots victory is now a model for communities across Latin America resisting fossil-fuel pressure.

item.title: French Polynesian Resort Protects 120,000 Sea Turtle Hatchlings
item.description: A French Polynesian resort has partnered with marine conservationists to protect over 120,000 sea turtle hatchlings on its beaches, transforming tourism into a direct force for ocean recovery. Guests are invited to witness nesting and hatching events, building a deep connection between visitors and one of the ocean's most ancient creatures.

item.title: Papua New Guinea Activist Wins Global Prize for Battling Mine Pollution
item.description: Theonila Roka Matbob spent a decade challenging a mining company fouling the rivers and forests her community depended on — and won. A 2026 Goldman Environmental Prize recipient, her perseverance secured clean water and restored ecosystems for thousands of families across Papua New Guinea's affected highlands.

item.title: Saving Every Threatened Australian Species Is More Affordable Than Thought
item.description: A new analysis has found that protecting every single threatened species in Australia would cost far less than previous estimates suggested — making the investment achievable at a national policy level. Researchers say the finding removes a key economic argument against bold conservation and could unlock unprecedented species protection.

item.title: 3-Million-Year-Old Antarctic Ice Rewrites Climate Science
item.description: Scientists drilling at East Antarctica's Allan Hills extracted 3-million-year-old ice bubbles revealing that greenhouse gases stayed remarkably stable while Earth cooled dramatically — a puzzle that challenges long-held assumptions about what drives ice ages. The finding opens new avenues for understanding how our planet's climate system truly works.

item.title: Algeria Becomes the 29th Country to Eradicate Infectious Blindness
item.description: The World Health Organization has validated Algeria as the 29th nation to eliminate trachoma as a public health problem — the world’s leading cause of infectious blindness. The achievement caps decades of community health work reaching millions with antibiotics and hygiene education.

item.title: Zambia Ends Bail for Sexual Offence Suspects to Protect Survivors
item.description: Zambia’s Criminal Procedure Code Amendment Act now makes sexual offences including rape and child abuse non-bailable, requiring suspects to remain in custody until trial. Justice Minister Princess Kasune Zulu, a child-marriage survivor turned activist, championed the reform as a landmark step toward justice.

item.title: African Startups Raise a Record $705 Million in Q1 2026, Up 27%
item.description: Across 59 deals in the first quarter, African tech startups pulled in $705 million — a 26.5% jump year-on-year — with Egypt, South Africa and Kenya leading the charge. Google’s 10th Africa Accelerator cohort, chosen from 2,600 applicants, spotlights AI-first founders tackling local challenges.

item.title: Laos–China Railway Clocks 800,000 Cross-Border Journeys on Its Third Anniversary
item.description: Three years after its cross-border passenger launch, the Laos–China Railway has carried more than 800,000 international travelers between Vientiane and Kunming — a journey now completed in under 10 hours instead of several days. Tourism corridors have widened and freight has surged, reshaping Southeast Asian connectivity.

item.title: ADB Names India the World’s Fastest-Growing Major Economy at 6.9%
item.description: The Asian Development Bank’s April 2026 outlook forecasts India’s GDP expanding 6.9% in fiscal year 2026 — the highest rate among all major economies — powered by robust domestic demand, resilient exports and a favourable trade environment.

item.title: Zhao Xintong Opens World Title Defence With Stunning Form After Comeback
item.description: Defending snooker world champion Zhao Xintong opened the 2026 World Championship at the Crucible with a 10–7 first-round win, continuing an electrifying season in which he swept three ranking titles. His return from a 20-month match-fixing suspension has become one of sport’s most compelling comebacks.

item.title: UK Electric Cars Undercut Petrol Prices for the Very First Time
item.description: The average new electric vehicle in Britain now costs £42,620 — £785 less than the average new petrol car — marking a historic milestone driven by sustained manufacturer discounts and government grants. Electric vehicles accounted for 22% of new car registrations in Q1 2026, up 14.5% year-on-year.

item.title: Golden Eagles Set to Soar Over England Again After 150 Years Away
item.description: The UK government has committed £1 million to explore reintroducing golden eagles to England, where Victorian-era persecution wiped them out entirely. Eight potential recovery zones across northern England have been identified, and satellite-tagged birds from Scotland have already begun scouting the border regions.

item.title: European Bison Rebounds From Near-Extinction to Over 9,000 Animals
item.description: A century after just 60 individuals survived in captivity, the European bison population has climbed above 9,000 free-roaming animals — a tenfold increase in a decade. A new study confirms the herds now sequester carbon equivalent to offsetting 84,000 petrol cars each year.

item.title: Printed Artificial Neurons Successfully Communicate With Living Brain Cells
item.description: Northwestern University engineers used aerosol jet printing to create flexible, ink-based artificial neurons that generate lifelike electrical signals and trigger real responses in mouse brain tissue. Published in Nature Nanotechnology, the breakthrough opens a path toward brain-machine interfaces that could restore hearing, vision and movement.

item.title: Cornell Scientists Take a Giant Step Toward Reversible Male Birth Control
item.description: A Cornell University study shows a small molecule targeting a checkpoint in sperm-cell division completely halts sperm production in mice, with full fertility restored within six weeks of stopping treatment. The nonhormonal approach sidesteps the side effects that stalled every previous male contraceptive effort.

item.title: Michigan Wolverines Win 2026 NCAA Title, Ending a 37-Year Championship Drought
item.description: The University of Michigan men’s basketball team defeated UConn 69–63 to claim the 2026 national championship — the program’s first title since 1989. They posted the highest net rating of any champion in KenPom history, ending a 37-year wait for jubilant fans.

item.title: Peru Welcomes 823,000 International Visitors in Q1 2026 as Tourism Rebounds
item.description: Peru recorded 823,863 international arrivals in Q1 2026 — up 3.5% year-on-year — with Chile topping the source market list for the first time. Projections suggest Peru could welcome 4 million foreign tourists by year-end, moving the sector close to pre-pandemic heights.

item.title: Argentina Breaks Its Inflation Spiral as Economy Grows 4.4% in 2025
item.description: After years of triple-digit inflation, Argentina’s annual price growth has fallen to around 31% — its lowest in a decade — while GDP grew 4.4% in 2025. The IMF called the stabilisation one of the most successful in recent memory, and international reserves are finally turning positive.

item.title: Chile on Track to Protect Over Half Its Waters, Surpassing Global Target
item.description: Chile is set to bring more than 50% of its territorial waters under formal protection — nearly double the 30-by-2030 global target that 190 nations pledged to pursue. The commitment safeguards extraordinary marine biodiversity across some of the most pristine ocean in the Southern Hemisphere.

item.title: New Zealand Launches Bold ‘Action for Nature’ Plan to Restore Wildlife
item.description: Conservation Minister Tama Potaka unveiled the ‘Action for Nature’ implementation plan on April 23, setting a clear framework for smarter investment, stronger community partnerships and targeted protection of the species and habitats that need help most. The plan aims to maximise every conservation dollar’s impact.

item.title: Oceania Targets 84% Renewable Electricity by 2035 as Solar and Wind Surge
item.description: A new analysis finds Oceania’s exceptional solar and wind resources could supply 84% of the region’s electricity needs by 2035 — up from roughly 60% in 2025. Pacific island nations are already running some of the world’s highest renewable energy shares, pioneering the clean energy transition.

item.title: Pacific Island Nations Unite Around 100% Renewable Vision in Tassiriki Call
item.description: Pacific Island states have issued the Tassiriki Call — a landmark unified declaration backing 100% renewable energy and an urgent fossil fuel phase-out ahead of a major international conference in Colombia. The declaration amplifies the Pacific’s moral authority as the world’s most climate-frontline communities.

item.title: Scientists Stumble Upon an Uncharted Island in Antarctica’s Weddell Sea
item.description: While sheltering from a storm in the Weddell Sea, 93 scientists aboard Germany’s Polarstern icebreaker discovered a previously unmapped island — 130 metres long and 16 metres above sea level — whose ice-covered surface had fooled satellites for decades. The expedition team will officially name the new island.

item.title: Africa's First Binding Road Safety Charter Enters Into Force
item.description: After Mozambique became the 15th nation to ratify it, Africa's continental Road Safety Charter officially entered into force on March 26 — legally binding signatories to halve road deaths and injuries by 2030. The WHO called it a milestone for a continent where 250,000 people die on roads every year. Fifteen countries now share enforceable commitments to safer streets across Africa.

item.title: Kenya Opens World's Largest Black Rhino Sanctuary at Tsavo West
item.description: Kenya unveiled the expanded Tsavo West Rhino Sanctuary in December 2025, stretching across 3,200 square kilometres — the largest rhino sanctuary on Earth. The site now shelters 200 black rhinos, the biggest single population in Kenya, and positions the country as guardian of 78% of the world's Eastern black rhinos. By 2030, the sanctuary is expected to generate over $45 million a year in conservation tourism.

item.title: Off-Grid Solar Lights Up 11,000 Women-Owned Businesses in Nigeria
item.description: A World Bank-backed initiative connecting distributed solar energy to underserved Nigerian communities has electrified more than 11,000 women-owned enterprises, transforming subsistence work into profitable businesses. Women who joined solar assembly plants as entry-level workers have advanced to certified installers and sales agents earning stable incomes while electrifying their neighbours. Rice miller Rashida, for example, now processes up to 100 bags at a time — compared with hand-to-mouth survival before the solar connection.

item.title: Vietnam Leads ASEAN With a 7.83% Economic Surge in Early 2026
item.description: Vietnam's economy grew 7.83% year-on-year in Q1 2026 — the fastest rate in Southeast Asia and well above government targets. Strong manufacturing exports and rising domestic consumption drove the expansion, with the National Statistics Office calling it one of the best first-quarter performances in the country's recent history. The result cements Vietnam's standing as one of the Asia-Pacific's most dynamic emerging economies.

item.title: India Commissions a Record 64,895 MW of New Power Capacity in a Single Year
item.description: India added 64,895 megawatts of new power generation capacity across all sources in fiscal year 2025–26, the highest annual total in the country's history and roughly double the pace of just five years ago. The surge — led by solar and wind — pushed India to third place globally in renewable installed capacity and helped the country meet its 50% non-fossil capacity target five years ahead of the 2030 deadline. Officials say the milestone marks a turning point in India's energy transformation.

item.title: Japan's Nikkei 225 Closes at All-Time High as AI Stocks Soar
item.description: Japan's benchmark Nikkei 225 closed at a record 59,585 on April 22, lifted by surging AI and technology stocks as investor confidence in Japan's semiconductor ambitions reached new heights. The milestone came weeks after Japan approved ¥631.5 billion in subsidies for domestic AI chipmaker Rapidus and Microsoft announced a $10 billion commitment to Japanese AI infrastructure. Analysts say the rally reflects a broad global recognition of Japan's technology renaissance.

item.title: London's Long-Awaited V&A East Museum Opens in Stratford
item.description: The Victoria & Albert Museum's eagerly awaited East London branch opened on April 18, bringing world-class design, art, and culture to the Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park in Stratford. Its inaugural exhibition, 'The Music Is Black: A British Story,' traces 125 years of Black music-making in Britain through instruments, fashion, and photography. Two free permanent galleries showcase over 500 objects spanning global art, architecture, and fashion — free to all visitors.

item.title: Europe's Electric Vehicle Sales Surge 29% in Q1 — 51% in March Alone
item.description: Sales of electric vehicles across Europe rose 29.4% in Q1 2026 compared with the same period last year, with growth accelerating to 51.3% in March — the fastest monthly increase on record. New affordable models and expanded charging infrastructure are fuelling the boom, with multiple markets hitting historic market-share highs. Europe's EV momentum is seen as a major tailwind for the continent's climate and clean air targets.

item.title: UK Households Can Now Earn Free Electricity When the Grid Has Too Much
item.description: Britain's National Energy System Operator launched a scheme this spring allowing households to earn free or heavily discounted electricity during periods when renewable generation outpaces demand. The programme gives consumers a direct financial stake in the clean-energy transition and prevents surplus wind and solar power from going to waste. It is the first initiative of its kind at national scale in the UK and is expected to benefit millions of smart-meter households.

item.title: 6,500 Volunteers Build 10,000 Beds in 24 Hours for Kids Without One
item.description: In Charlotte, North Carolina, 6,500 volunteers from Lowe's, Bank of America, and the Charlotte Hornets assembled 10,027 wooden beds in a single 24-hour blitz — using 200 miles of lumber and nearly 730,000 screws. The beds, built for Sleep in Heavenly Peace charity, will be distributed across 36 states to children who currently sleep on the floor. More than 140,000 children in the US remain on the charity's waiting list for a bed of their own.

item.title: 30,000 Endangered Coho Salmon Return to California River — Double the Record
item.description: More than 30,000 endangered Central California coast coho salmon returned to the Russian River during the 2024–2025 spawning season, doubling the previous record of 15,000 and signalling a stunning river restoration success. NOAA Fisheries credited decades of habitat work, egg protection programmes, and reduced fishing pressure for the turnaround. Scientists say the coho's return is transforming the entire river ecosystem as a cascade of life follows them home.

item.title: North America's Largest Wildlife Overpass Opens in Colorado, Linking 39,000 Acres
item.description: The I-25 Greenland wildlife overpass near Larkspur, Colorado opened in December 2025, spanning six lanes of interstate highway to connect 39,000 acres of critical habitat. Elk, pronghorn, mule deer, black bears, and mountain lions are already using the crossing, according to early camera-trap monitoring. The overpass is North America's largest and follows decades of advocacy by wildlife biologists who documented thousands of animal deaths on the highway.

item.title: Amazon Deforestation Drops 35%, Placing Brazil on Track for Historic Low
item.description: Satellite data shows Amazon deforestation fell 35% between August 2025 and January 2026 compared to the prior year, reaching its lowest level since 2014. If the trend holds through the year, Environment Minister Marina Silva says 2026 could mark the lowest annual deforestation rate since records began in 1988. Stronger enforcement and the participation of 70 high-deforestation municipalities in federal prevention programmes are credited with driving the change.

item.title: Brazil Awards Its First Amazon Reforestation Concession to a Green Startup
item.description: Brazil granted its first-ever long-term public land concession specifically for Amazon reforestation to startup Re.green in March 2026, giving the company rights to restore 59,000 hectares of degraded land in the Bom Futuro National Forest over 40 years. The deal is backed by carbon finance and is seen as a landmark test case for scaling private investment in tropical forest recovery. Experts say it opens a potential $10–20 billion revenue window for Amazon conservation this decade.

item.title: Colombia Protects Nearly Half Its Ocean, Hitting 30x30 Target Five Years Early
item.description: Colombia has placed 47% of its exclusive economic zone under marine conservation, surpassing the global 30x30 goal five years ahead of the 2030 deadline. The OECD's 2026 Environmental Performance Review celebrated the achievement as one of Latin America's most significant conservation milestones. Colombia's payments for ecosystem services and community-led biodiversity offsets are now being studied as a replicable model for other tropical nations.

item.title: Cook Islands and New Zealand Sign Security Pact, Unlocking $29.8M for Pacific Stability
item.description: New Zealand and the Cook Islands formalised a landmark defence and security agreement in April 2026, with New Zealand resuming $29.8 million in support for Cook Islands' long-term sovereignty and resilience. The deal deepens ties between the two closely connected nations at a time of growing strategic interest in the Pacific. Officials said the pact reflects a shared commitment to a peaceful, self-determined, and climate-resilient Pacific future.

item.title: Australia's Beloved Bilby Records One of Its Biggest Population Boosts Ever
item.description: The Greater Bilby — Australia's endangered 'Easter bunny of the outback' — recorded one of its largest-ever population increases, with thriving numbers confirmed at protected sites across Central Australia as of April 2026. The Australian Wildlife Conservancy celebrated by releasing a healthy, recovered bilby back onto Ngalia Walpiri and Luritja Country on April 16. Conservation fencing, predator controls, and community ranger programmes are credited with turning the tide for this beloved marsupial.

item.title: New Zealand Pledges $20 Million to Help Tuvalu Prepare for Rising Seas
item.description: New Zealand committed $20 million to strengthen Tuvalu's resilience against flooding and sea-level rise, announced alongside a broader bilateral security partnership in April 2026. The funding will support coastal infrastructure, early-warning systems, and community climate adaptation for one of the Pacific's most vulnerable nations. The commitment deepens New Zealand's role as a leading partner in Pacific climate security.

item.title: Scientists Drill Record 228-Metre Core From Beneath Antarctica's Ice Sheet
item.description: A team co-led by Imperial College London extracted the longest sediment core ever drilled from beneath an Antarctic ice sheet — a 228-metre column of ancient mud and rock spanning 23 million years of climate history. Researchers bored through 523 metres of ice at the remote Crary Ice Rise site to reach the sediment, finding the first in-situ evidence of open-ocean conditions in West Antarctica's interior. The core will help scientists understand how past warming reshaped the ice sheet and predict future sea-level rise.
